title: Vision: Relay Multi-Agent Next Pass
description:
## Vision
What comes after v1.0 first pass — making it trivially easy to set up multiple agents with different purposes, without heavy infrastructure. The central tension: should agent identity live in project CLAUDE.md files, or in Relay's own config?

## Clarity
Loose — User is explicitly thinking out loud and wants concept options developed. The 'where does agent identity live' question is genuinely open.

title: Research multi-agent patterns from proposal
description:
## Goal
Pull the relevant multi-agent and multi-project patterns from the original research proposal that apply to the next pass.

## Details
- Re-read the research proposal for multi-project routing, per-project config, agent lifecycle
- Re-read the exec summary for the config/routing architecture
- Identify what's worth pulling into the next pass vs what's over-engineering
- Focus on: how easy is it to add a new agent? What's the minimum config?

title: Develop concept options for agent identity model
description:
## Goal
Present 2-3 distinct approaches for where agent identity/purpose lives, with tradeoffs for each.

## Details
The core tension:
- Option space: CLAUDE.md-only (project owns identity) vs Relay-owned prompts vs hybrid
- Must consider: what happens when you add a new agent? How many files do you touch? How many places define 'who the agent is'?
- Must consider: isaac_research is being retired as the test project — the system needs to work for arbitrary future projects
- Must consider: the user wants personality/purpose customization without touching project repos

title: Write vision document
description:
## Goal
Produce the loose-format vision doc with developed options for the multi-agent next pass.

## Details
- Loose format: distilled intent, multiple options with tradeoffs, open questions
- Include what to pull from the research proposal
- Include the agent identity options
- Include what the 'add a new agent' workflow looks like under each option
- Include what gets deferred further

title: Vision: Relay v1.0 First Pass
description:
## Vision
Define the concrete v1.0 scope for Relay — a single-agent Telegram bot with session persistence, purpose.md-driven agent spawning, and an intake router. Tighten the loose multi-project/multi-platform vision into a first-pass implementation spec.

## Clarity
Mixed — The v1.0 definition (single agent, Telegram, session resume, purpose.md) is lucid, but the routing agent concept, config shape, and agent ergonomics integration need development.

title: Research existing codebase patterns and CLI flags
description:
## Goal
Ground the vision in the actual codebase state and Claude Code CLI capabilities.

## Details
- Scan the repo for any existing code, configs, or structure
- Verify Claude Code CLI flags (--resume, --allowedTools, --output-format json, --append-system-prompt)
- Note what exists vs what needs building

title: Develop v1.0 scope and architecture proposal
description:
## Goal
Define what's in/out for v1.0, propose the minimal architecture, and specify the intake router concept.

## Details
- Distill the user's v1.0 definition into concrete requirements
- Propose config shape for single-agent setup
- Define the intake router: what it does, how smart it needs to be
- Specify purpose.md and agent project directory conventions
- Define session management approach (resume vs new)
- Address vox integration for voice

title: Write vision document
description:
## Goal
Produce the final vision document with polished v1.0 spec, grounded in research and codebase context.

## Details
- Polish the user's clear parts (Telegram, single agent, session resume)
- Develop concrete proposal for the loose parts (routing agent, config, agent ergonomics)
- Include codebase references where relevant
- List what's deferred to v1.1+

title: Relay v1.0: Telegram-to-Claude Agent
description:
## Overview
Build Relay v1.0 — a Telegram bot that routes messages to a Claude Code agent working in ~/isaac_research. Session persistence by default, voice transcription, intake router for meta-commands. Agent uses the project's native CLAUDE.md and .claude/ setup.

## Phases
- Phase A: Research & Planning (3 tasks)
- Phase B: Implementation (12 tasks — 6 write/review pairs)
- Phase C: Integration & Testing (3 tasks)

## Deliverables
1. src/relay/store.py — SQLite operations
2. src/relay/config.py — YAML config loading
3. src/relay/voice.py — Voice transcription (vox + OpenAI fallback)
4. src/relay/agent.py — Claude subprocess management + session lifecycle
5. src/relay/intake.py — Intake router/classifier
6. src/relay/telegram.py — Telegram adapter
7. src/relay/main.py — Entry point
8. relay.yaml — Configuration template
9. relay.service — Systemd unit file
10. tests/ — Test suite
11. requirements.txt + pyproject.toml — Project setup

title: Phase A: Research & Planning
description:
## Agent Model
A single agent works through A.1 → A.2 → A.3 sequentially. A.1 investigates Claude CLI behavior. A.2 produces the implementation spec. A.3 reviews and approves.

## Sub-tasks
- A.1: Investigate Claude CLI subprocess behavior with --resume
- A.2: Write implementation specification
- A.3: Review implementation specification

## Completion Criteria
Implementation spec approved — all Phase B agents can reference it to build their modules independently.
close_reason: All 3 tasks complete: CLI research, implementation spec, spec review

title: A.1: Investigate Claude CLI subprocess behavior
description:
## Objective
Test and document Claude Code CLI behavior for the subprocess patterns Relay needs.

## Research Questions
1. How does `claude --resume <session_id> -p <message> --output-format json` behave?
   - What JSON structure is returned? Where is session_id in the output?
   - What happens on first call (no prior session)?
   - What happens when resuming an expired/invalid session?
2. How does `--allowedTools` interact with `-p` mode?
   - Can we pass a list like `--allowedTools Read Glob Grep Edit Write Bash`?
3. How does `--append-system-prompt` work alongside CLAUDE.md?
   - Does CLAUDE.md still load when using `-p` mode with a `cwd`?
4. What does the existing isaac_research telegram bot look like?
   - Read `~/isaac_research/services/telegram_bot.py` for subprocess patterns to preserve
5. Does `--session-id <uuid>` let us assign deterministic session IDs?

## Output
Write findings to `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/cli-research.md`

## Files to Read
- `~/isaac_research/services/telegram_bot.py` — existing bot subprocess pattern
- `~/isaac_research/CLAUDE.md` — project CLAUDE.md for testing
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — vision for context
close_reason: CLI research complete — all 5 questions answered with evidence, subprocess pattern documented

title: A.2: Write implementation specification
description:
## Objective
Produce a detailed implementation spec that Phase B sub-agents can reference independently. Each module's spec must be self-sufficient — a sub-agent reading only its section should know exactly what to build.

## Input
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — architecture decisions
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/cli-research.md` — CLI behavior findings from A.1
- `research/isaac-telegram-bot-analysis.md` — patterns to carry forward
- `research/2026-03-01--vox-evaluation/` — vox integration details

## Design Change: CLAUDE.md replaces purpose.md
The agent uses the project's native CLAUDE.md and .claude/ setup. Relay spawns Claude with cwd=project_dir. Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md automatically. Relay only adds `--append-system-prompt` for Relay-specific context (mobile reading, concise responses). The relay.yaml config points to project_dir — it does NOT define the agent's system prompt.

## Spec Must Cover (per module)
For each of the 6 modules (store, config, voice, agent, intake, telegram):
- Exact file path
- Public interface (function signatures with types)
- Internal implementation details
- Dependencies on other modules
- Error handling approach
- Test expectations

## Additional Specs
- relay.yaml schema (with isaac_research as the configured project)
- SQLite schema (exact CREATE TABLE statements)
- Project scaffolding (pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, directory structure)
- systemd service file

## Output
Write to `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md`
close_reason: Implementation spec written — 1300+ lines, all 6 modules fully specified, reviewed and revised

title: A.3: Review implementation specification
description:
## Objective
Review the implementation spec from A.2. Verify it is complete, consistent, and that each module section is self-sufficient for a sub-agent to implement independently.

## Review Criteria
1. Every module has: file path, public interface with typed signatures, implementation details, deps, error handling, test expectations
2. Module interfaces are consistent — types used in one module's output match what the consuming module expects as input
3. SQLite schema matches the CRUD operations defined in store.py's interface
4. relay.yaml schema is complete and defaults to isaac_research project
5. No circular dependencies between modules
6. The CLAUDE.md approach is correctly specified (cwd-based, not --system-prompt)
7. Voice fallback (vox → OpenAI) is clearly specified
8. Intake router JSON schema is defined

## Input
`.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md`

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>
close_reason: Spec review passed — all 8 criteria verified, module interfaces consistent, no circular deps

title: Phase B: Implementation
description:
## Agent Model
Phase agent dispatches 6 write/review pairs. Write tasks are assigned to sub-agents that work in parallel (where dependencies allow). Review tasks are assigned to review agents that check output against the implementation spec.

### Parallel Groups
- **Group 1 (fully parallel):** store.py, config.py, voice.py — no internal deps
- **Group 2 (after store):** agent.py — needs store for session CRUD
- **Group 3 (after agent):** intake.py — needs agent interface to forward messages
- **Group 4 (after all):** telegram.py — wires everything together

## Sub-tasks
- B.1/B.2: Write/Review store.py
- B.3/B.4: Write/Review config.py
- B.5/B.6: Write/Review voice.py
- B.7/B.8: Write/Review agent.py
- B.9/B.10: Write/Review intake.py
- B.11/B.12: Write/Review telegram.py

## Completion Criteria
All 6 modules written and reviewed. Each module passes its review gate.

title: B.1: Write store.py
description:
## Objective
Build the SQLite storage module — session CRUD, message logging, database initialization.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — store.py section
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — SQLite schema

## Implementation Notes
- SQLite with WAL mode for concurrent access
- aiosqlite for async operations
- Tables: sessions, messages, config_state
- Session CRUD: create, get_active_by_chat_id, update_last_active, expire, close
- Message logging: log_message, get_messages_by_session
- Database init: create tables if not exist
- All timestamps in UTC

## Output
`src/relay/store.py`

title: B.10: Review intake.py
description:
## Objective
Review intake.py for correctness and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/intake.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Regex fast-path catches common commands without a Claude call
2. Claude classifier prompt is clear and produces valid JSON
3. All 4 actions handled correctly (forward, new_session, status, unclear)
4. Uses haiku model for cost efficiency
5. Graceful handling of malformed classifier output (falls back to forward)
6. No user message content leaked into subprocess commands unsafely

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.11: Write telegram.py
description:
## Objective
Build the Telegram adapter — polling loop, authorization, message handling, response chunking, voice download.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — telegram.py section
- `research/isaac-telegram-bot-analysis.md` — existing bot patterns (entire file)
- `~/isaac_research/services/telegram_bot.py` — reference implementation

## Implementation Notes
- python-telegram-bot library (async, polling mode)
- Authorization: fail-closed user ID allowlist from config
- Text handler: download text → intake router → agent (if forward) → chunk response → send
- Voice handler: download OGG bytes → voice.py transcribe → send 'Heard: {preview}' → intake router → agent → send response
- Response chunking at 4096 chars (Telegram limit)
- 'Thinking...' status message while agent processes (use ChatAction.TYPING)
- Error handling: catch agent timeouts, transcription failures, send user-friendly error messages
- Wires together: config, store, voice, intake, agent

## Output
`src/relay/telegram.py`

title: B.12: Review telegram.py
description:
## Objective
Review telegram.py for correctness, security, and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/telegram.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Polling loop setup is correct (Application.builder pattern)
2. Authorization is fail-closed (unauthorized messages silently dropped)
3. Text and voice handlers follow the spec flow
4. Response chunking at 4096 chars
5. Voice download → voice.py → intake → agent pipeline is correct
6. 'Heard: {preview}' message sent before agent processing
7. Error messages are user-friendly (no stack traces to Telegram)
8. All module integrations use the correct interfaces from the spec
9. No bot token leaked in logs or error messages

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.2: Review store.py
description:
## Objective
Review store.py for correctness, completeness, and adherence to the implementation spec.

## Input
`src/relay/store.py`

## Review Criteria
1. SQLite schema matches spec (sessions, messages, config_state tables)
2. WAL mode enabled at connection time
3. All CRUD operations match the public interface from the spec
4. Async operations use aiosqlite correctly
5. Proper error handling (database locked, missing tables, etc.)
6. Timestamps are UTC
7. No SQL injection vulnerabilities (parameterized queries only)

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.3: Write config.py
description:
## Objective
Build the YAML configuration loader. Reads relay.yaml, resolves environment variables, validates required fields.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — config.py section
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — relay.yaml schema

## Implementation Notes
- Single relay.yaml file
- Environment variable substitution (${VAR_NAME} syntax)
- Dataclass or typed dict for config structure
- Validation: required fields, type checking, path existence for project_dir
- Default values for timeout (900), session_ttl (14400), model (sonnet)
- Config for isaac_research as default project

## Output
`src/relay/config.py`

title: B.4: Review config.py
description:
## Objective
Review config.py for correctness and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/config.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Loads relay.yaml correctly
2. Environment variable substitution works (${VAR} pattern)
3. Required field validation with clear error messages
4. Default values match spec
5. Config dataclass/structure is clean and typed
6. Path validation for project_dir

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.5: Write voice.py
description:
## Objective
Build voice transcription module using vox as the sole transcription backend.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — voice.py section
- `research/2026-03-01--vox-evaluation/findings.md` — vox evaluation
- `research/2026-03-01--vox-evaluation/feature-request.md` — vox integration sketch

## Implementation Notes
- Use `vox file <path> --json --format=ogg` as subprocess
- Stdin support available: `vox file - --json --format=ogg` (pipe audio bytes directly)
- vox handles chunking internally (>8 min → 5-min chunks) — no chunking logic needed in Relay
- Input: audio bytes + format hint (ogg from Telegram)
- Output: transcribed text string
- Parse JSON output: {"text": "...", "duration": ..., "chunks": ...}
- Exit codes: 0=success, 1=general error, 2=API error, 3=no key
- Timeout: 5 minutes for transcription
- 'Heard: {preview}' pattern — return both preview and full text
- Requires: vox binary on PATH, OPENAI_API_KEY in environment

## Output
`src/relay/voice.py`

title: B.6: Review voice.py
description:
## Objective
Review voice.py for correctness and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/voice.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Vox subprocess call is correct (`vox file <path> --json --format=ogg`)
2. JSON output parsed correctly (text, duration, chunks fields)
3. Exit code handling maps to appropriate errors (0/1/2/3)
4. Temp file cleanup happens in all code paths (including errors) — or uses stdin pipe to avoid temp files entirely
5. Timeout handling (5 min)
6. Returns both preview (first 100 chars) and full text
7. Clear error message if vox is not installed (binary not on PATH)

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.7: Write agent.py
description:
## Objective
Build Claude Code subprocess management — session lifecycle, --resume handling, response parsing.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — agent.py section
- `research/isaac-telegram-bot-analysis.md` — subprocess patterns (lines 88-117)
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/cli-research.md` — CLI behavior findings

## Implementation Notes
- Spawn Claude: `claude --resume <session_id> -p <message> --output-format json --allowedTools <tools> --append-system-prompt <relay_context>`
- First message for new session: use `--session-id <uuid>` instead of --resume (or let claude generate the session_id from json output)
- cwd = project_dir from config (Claude reads CLAUDE.md automatically)
- Parse JSON output to extract: response text, session_id, cost info
- start_new_session=True for process group management
- env.pop('CLAUDECODE') to prevent token leakage
- Timeout via asyncio.wait_for + SIGTERM to process group on timeout
- Depends on store.py for session_id lookup and update

## Output
`src/relay/agent.py`

title: B.8: Review agent.py
description:
## Objective
Review agent.py for correctness, security, and adherence to spec.

## Input
`src/relay/agent.py`

## Review Criteria
1. Subprocess spawning matches the proven isaac_research pattern
2. --resume / --session-id usage is correct per CLI research findings
3. --allowedTools used instead of --dangerously-skip-permissions
4. Process group management (start_new_session=True, SIGTERM cleanup)
5. Environment isolation (CLAUDECODE removed)
6. JSON output parsing handles all expected fields
7. Session lifecycle integrates correctly with store.py interface
8. Timeout handling is robust (no orphaned processes)
9. No command injection via user message content

## Output
Verdict: pass | revise: <specific issues>

title: B.9: Write intake.py
description:
## Objective
Build the intake router — a lightweight Claude classifier that decides what to do with each incoming message before it reaches the agent.

## Research Files
- `.aura/plans/queue/relay-v1-build/implementation-spec.md` — intake.py section
- `.aura/visions/relay-v1-first-pass/vision.md` — intake router design

## Implementation Notes
- Fast-path regex for obvious commands ('start over', 'new session', '/new', '/status')
- Fallback: single `claude -p` call with small system prompt + --output-format json
- Classification output: {"action": "forward" | "new_session" | "status" | "unclear", "cleaned_message": "..."}
- Model: haiku (fast, cheap) for the classifier call
- Stateless — no session context needed for classification
- Handle the 'forward' case by returning the cleaned message for agent.py to process
- Handle 'new_session' by calling store to create new session, return confirmation text
- Handle 'status' by querying store for session info, return formatted text
- Handle 'unclear' by returning a brief help message

## Output
`src/relay/intake.py`

title: Phase C: Integration & Testing
description:
## Agent Model
Phase agent handles integration (main.py + config + service file), then dispatches test writing and live validation.

## Sub-tasks
- C.1: Build main.py, relay.yaml template, relay.service, project scaffolding
- C.2: Write automated tests (unit + integration)
- C.3: Live validation — user sets up Telegram bot, sends text and voice messages

## Completion Criteria
All tests pass. User has successfully sent text and voice messages through Relay to the isaac_research agent and verified session persistence.

title: C.1: Build main.py, relay.yaml, relay.service, project scaffolding
description:
## Objective
Create the entry point, configuration template, systemd service file, and project scaffolding (pyproject.toml, requirements.txt, __init__.py files).

## Deliverables
1. `src/relay/__init__.py` — package init
2. `src/relay/main.py` — entry point that loads config, initializes store, starts Telegram polling
3. `relay.yaml.example` — configuration template with isaac_research defaults
4. `relay.service` — systemd unit file (copy pattern from ~/isaac_research/services/telegram-bot.service)
5. `pyproject.toml` or `requirements.txt` — dependencies: python-telegram-bot>=21.0, aiosqlite>=0.19.0, pyyaml>=6.0
6. `.gitignore` — relay.db, relay.yaml (has secrets), __pycache__, .env

## Implementation Notes
- main.py is glue: load config, init store (create tables), build Telegram app, run polling
- Graceful shutdown: handle SIGTERM/SIGINT, close store connection
- Logging: Python logging to stdout (systemd captures it)
- relay.yaml.example configured for isaac_research:
  - project_dir: /home/ubuntu/isaac_research
  - allowed_tools: Read, Glob, Grep, Edit, Write, Bash, Agent
  - model: sonnet

title: C.2: Write automated tests
description:
## Objective
Write unit tests for each module and an integration test for the core message loop.

## Deliverables
`tests/` directory with:
- `test_store.py` — SQLite CRUD operations, session expiry, message logging
- `test_config.py` — YAML loading, env var substitution, validation errors
- `test_voice.py` — mock vox subprocess, mock OpenAI fallback, temp file cleanup
- `test_agent.py` — mock Claude subprocess, session resume logic, timeout handling
- `test_intake.py` — regex fast-path, mock classifier, action handling
- `test_integration.py` — mock Telegram update → full pipeline → response (end-to-end with mocks)

## Implementation Notes
- Use pytest + pytest-asyncio
- Mock external processes (claude, vox) with unittest.mock
- Use in-memory SQLite for store tests
- Test the real message flow: text in → intake classify → agent spawn → response out
- Test session persistence: two messages in same chat, verify --resume used on second
- Test 'start over': verify new session created
- Test voice: mock audio bytes → mock vox → verify transcript forwarded

## Testing Against isaac_research
For integration tests that can run live (not mocked), stage tests that:
- Ask Claude about existing research in ~/isaac_research (e.g., 'What research topics exist?')
- Verify Claude can read files in the repo
- Verify session persistence across two sequential calls
These are optional/manual tests, not part of CI.

title: C.3: Live validation — Telegram bot end-to-end test
description:
## Objective
User sets up a new Telegram bot and validates the full Relay system end-to-end.

## Prerequisites
- All modules built and reviewed (Phase B complete)
- main.py, relay.yaml, relay.service in place (C.1 complete)
- Automated tests passing (C.2 complete)

## Validation Steps

### Setup
1. User creates a new Telegram bot via @BotFather (or reuses existing)
2. Copy relay.yaml.example to relay.yaml, fill in bot token and user ID
3. Install dependencies: pip install -r requirements.txt (or pip install -e .)
4. Initialize: python -m relay.main (verify DB created, polling starts)

### Test 1: Text Message
- Send a text message to the bot: 'What research topics exist in this repo?'
- Verify: bot responds with information from ~/isaac_research
- Verify: session created in relay.db

### Test 2: Session Persistence
- Send a follow-up: 'Tell me more about the first topic'
- Verify: Claude has context from the previous message (--resume working)
- Verify: same session_id used

### Test 3: New Session
- Send: 'Start over'
- Verify: bot confirms new session
- Send: 'What was I just asking about?'
- Verify: Claude has NO context from previous session

### Test 4: Voice Message
- Send a voice message asking about the research
- Verify: bot sends 'Heard: ...' preview
- Verify: bot responds with agent answer

### Test 5: Session TTL (optional)
- Wait for session to expire (or temporarily set TTL to 60 seconds)
- Send a new message
- Verify: new session created automatically

## Output
Validation report: which tests passed/failed, any issues discovered.
